perturbation.original_text: 4. Mandatory Disclosure. In the event that the Recipient or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confident ial Information, the Recipient shall give prompt notice so that the Discloser may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief. In the event that such protective order is not obtained, the Recipient shall disclose only that portion of the Confidenti al Information which its counsel advises that it is legally required to disclose.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be binding upon and for the benefit o f the undersigned parties, their successors and assigns, provided that Confidential Information may not be assigned without the prior written consent of the Discloser. Failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of any term hereof. In the event that the Recipient or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confident ial Information, the Recipient shall give prompt notice so that the Discloser may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief. In the event that such protective order is not obtained, the Recipient shall disclose only that portion of the Confidenti al Information which its counsel advises that it is legally required to disclose.
perturbation.explanation: The mandatory disclosure clause is moved to the 'Miscellaneous' section, making it less prominent and potentially overlooked. This is misleading as it does not follow standard legal practices, where mandatory disclosures are generally placed in a section of their own, easily found by legal professionals. This is a legal contradiction because mandatory disclosures are supposed to be placed separately to improve readability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Disclosure requirements under various legal frameworks related to confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of the GDPR requires that any processing of personal data must have a lawful basis. Moving the mandatory disclosure section violates this by obscuring data protection requirements in a way that could be argued as non-compliant with the principle of transparency, which is essential for lawful processing under the GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shal

perturbation.justification: The original text has been modified such that a mandatory disclosure clause has been moved to the 'Miscellaneous' section. The perturbation explanation states this makes it less prominent and potentially overlooked, and does not follow standard legal practices, where mandatory disclosures are generally placed in a section of their own. The Law Explanation states that this change violates GDPR by obscuring data protection requirements, conflicting with the principle of transparency. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that processing shall be lawful only if, inter alia, 'the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes', and 'processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject'. If the mandatory disclosure is obscured, consent cannot be fully obtained and legal obligations cannot be met, and therefore this move contradicts this part of GDPR, which is supported by the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that its obligations hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covena nts and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that its obligations hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be adequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covena nts and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser. For and on behalf of For and on be half of MMGRP Limited [ ] Print Name Print Name Position Position Date: Date: MMGRP Limited , 145 – 157 St Johns Stre et, London , EC1V 4PW Company Number: 07527443 DPA Registration Number: Z2553231 t. +44 (0) 207 873 2421 f. +44 (0) 844 443 2349 e. sales@mmgrp.co.uk
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows Discloser to obtain injunctive relief in case of breach without the necessity of proving actual damages, however, with the altered text monetary damages would be adequate to compensate Discloser. This creates uncertainty in the conditions under which injunctive relief can be sought.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law regarding Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 41(e) of the Specific Relief Act 1963 outlines instances where an injunction cannot be granted, including when there is an equally efficacious remedy available. By stating that monetary damages *would* be adequate, the modified text undermines the claim that injunctive relief is necessary, potentially barring the Discloser from seeking an injunction under this legal principle.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that monetary damages would be adequate, contradicting the original text that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser. The Law Explanation highlights that Section 41(e) of the Specific Relief Act 1963 may bar the Discloser from seeking an injunction if an equally efficacious remedy is available. By stating that monetary damages would be adequate, the changed text undermines the claim that injunctive relief is necessary, potentially creating a legal contradiction as the Discloser might not be able to obtain injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the United Kingdom, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the United States, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from the United Kingdom to the United States introduces a legal contradiction. The contract is initially drafted and intended to be used under UK laws. Altering the governing law, specifically, potentially invalidates the original intent of the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The Rome I Regulation governs contractual obligations within the EU. While the UK is no longer part of the EU, this regulation is based on international contract law principles. Changing the governing law to the United States may contradict principles of 'closest connection' and parties' intentions, potentially violating the underlying spirit of the Rome I Regulation, which prioritizes the law of the country most closely connected to the contract.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies UK law, while the modified version designates US law. The Perturbation Explanation states this introduces a legal contradiction because the initial contract's intent was based on UK laws, and changing the governing law potentially invalidates the original intent. The Law Explanation says this contradicts principles of 'closest connection' and parties' intentions, potentially violating the spirit of the Rome I Regulation, which prioritizes the law of the country most closely connected to the contract. Although the UK is not part of the EU, the explanation points to international contract law principles that may still apply. The Structural Flaws definition for Legal Contradiction includes instances where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret, which applies here, where the entire governing law is moved.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such doc umentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, but accompanied by copies of such doc umentation is only required if requested, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.explanation: The change allows the recipient to not include copies unless requested. This contrasts the original language which states the recipient will have to send copies of the documentation. If the recipient never requests copies, this clause might be abused.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. By allowing the Recipient to withhold copies unless specifically requested, the modification opens the door to opportunistic behavior. The Discloser must explicitly request copies, which may not happen due to oversight or lack of awareness. This subtle shift undermines the Discloser's ability to fully document the shared confidential information which is counter to good faith dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the obligation of good faith under UCC § 1-304. The original text mandates the recipient to return copies of documentation, whereas the changed text makes it optional, contingent on a request. This shift introduces an opportunity for the Recipient to potentially withhold copies, which could undermine the Discloser's ability to fully document shared confidential information. This undermines good faith dealing, as per the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement."
